label: Hyena
definition: A sub-quadratic sequence operator that replaces self-attention with a recurrence of long, implicitly parameterized convolutions interleaved with input-dependent gating, scaling near-linearly with sequence length while keeping a global receptive field.

body.text: Hyena is a sequence mixing operator designed as a drop-in alternative to self-attention that avoids its quadratic cost. It is built from two ingredients applied in alternation: very long convolutions, whose filters span the entire sequence, and element-wise multiplicative gating, where the signal is modulated by a projection of the input itself. Stacking several of these convolution-then-gate steps gives the Hyena operator. The long convolutions let information travel across the whole sequence in one layer, supplying the global receptive field that attention provides, while the gating introduces a data-dependent, multiplicative interaction that a plain convolution lacks.

The defining technical move is that the convolution filters are not stored as explicit weight vectors. A filter as long as the sequence would be enormous and would tie the model to a fixed maximum length. Instead Hyena parameterizes each filter implicitly, generating its values from a small neural network that maps a position to a filter weight. This implicit parameterization keeps the parameter count modest and independent of sequence length, lets the same model run at different lengths, and produces smooth filters that generalize well. The long convolutions themselves are evaluated efficiently with the fast Fourier transform, which computes a convolution in time that grows as the length times its logarithm rather than as the length squared.

Hyena matters because it showed that a carefully built convolutional operator, with no attention at all, can approach the language-modeling quality of transformers while scaling much better to long contexts. The gating is essential to that result: experiments showed that long convolutions alone fell short, and that the multiplicative, input-controlled interaction was what recovered the expressivity needed to match attention. As context lengths grow into the tens of thousands of tokens, Hyena's near-linear scaling opens a regime that quadratic attention cannot reach economically, which made it influential in long-sequence domains including genomics.

Hyena is a close cousin of the state space model family, and the relationship is direct. A structured state space layer also realizes a long convolution, just one whose kernel is generated by a linear recurrence rather than by a position network, so S4 and Hyena are two ways of building the same long-convolution primitive. Both stand alongside linear attention and the retentive network as members of the broader class of efficient sequence mixers seeking to replace all-pairs attention, and the gating idea at Hyena's heart reappears throughout that class, including in the selective mechanism of mamba.

image.alt: Diagram of the Hyena operator drawn as a recurrence: an input u is projected, then alternately passed through long-convolution matrices labeled S (shown as banded Toeplitz matrices) and data-controlled diagonal gating matrices labeled D, repeated several times to the output y; on the right, the long filters are generated by a small feed-forward network with a window and positional encoding.
image.caption: Hyena replaces self-attention with a recurrence of two cheap primitives applied in alternation: very long convolutions (the Toeplitz matrices S, giving a global receptive field) and element-wise multiplicative gating controlled by the input (the diagonal matrices D). The convolution filters are not stored explicitly but generated by a small feed-forward network from each position (right), which keeps the parameter count independent of sequence length and lets the long convolutions run efficiently with the fast Fourier transform.
